title: Reading the Clues
subtitle: Data: traditional, alternative, unstructured

hook.metaphor: Before you lend to someone, you gather clues. Do they have a steady job? Did they pay you back last time? A lender does the same thing — except its 'clues' are *data*. The better and broader the clues, the sharper the decision. The story of modern credit is the story of lenders finding new clues nobody used to look at.
hook.scenario: Two applicants want the same loan. One has a thin credit history (young, new to the country) but pays rent and phone bills on time for years. The other has a long credit history but it's patchy. The old system favours the second. Should the rent-and-bills clues count?

learn: For decades, credit decisions ran on **traditional data**: your credit score, your loan and repayment history, your income. Useful — but it leaves out millions of 'thin-file' people who are perfectly creditworthy but simply haven't borrowed before.

Enter **alternative data**: clues the old system ignored. Rent and utility payments, phone-bill history, **telematics** (how you actually drive, for car loans), even — in some markets — prescription data or **Electronic Health Records (EHRs)** for medical lending. These can reveal reliability that a credit score misses entirely. (You met one of these — 'rent paid on time' — as a slider on the Approval Dial.)

Then there's **unstructured data**: information that isn't in neat rows and columns. Customer-service call transcripts, app behaviour, even social-media sentiment. FinTechs mine these to spot shifts in borrower behaviour early — a flood of angry support calls can signal trouble before a payment is ever missed.
All this fuels **accelerated underwriting**: using real-time data to make a sound credit decision in seconds instead of weeks — *without* throwing away accuracy. More clues, gathered faster.

But more clues cut both ways. Alternative and unstructured data raise serious **privacy** questions: just because you *can* use someone's health records or social posts doesn't mean you should — or that it's legal. Australia's Privacy Act and the **Consumer Data Right (CDR)**, New Zealand's Privacy Act, India's **DPDP Act** and Account Aggregator consent framework, and Europe's GDPR all draw lines around what data a lender may use and how. Good lenders treat consent and fairness as part of the clue-gathering, not an afterthought.
bridge: So at work, you can always ask of any credit decision: *what clues is this resting on?* Traditional data alone? Alternative signals? Unstructured behaviour? And the follow-up that separates the responsible from the reckless: *did the borrower consent, and is using this data fair and legal?* Data is the fuel — but fuel needs handling rules.

interactions.title: Sort the clues
interactions.prompt: Drag each data source into the right kind of clue.

items.label: Credit score and past loan repayment history
Rent and utility bills paid on time
Declared income on the application form
Telematics: how safely someone actually drives

interactions.title: Should the new clues count?
interactions.prompt: Your lender could approve the thin-file applicant by using years of on-time rent and phone payments. What's the soundest, most responsible call?

options.label: Use the rent/bills data — with the applicant's consent.
options.feedback: This is the promise of alternative data done right: it includes a creditworthy person the old system would reject, *and* respects consent and privacy law. Inclusion + fairness.

options.label: Refuse — only a traditional credit score can be trusted.
options.feedback: This is exactly the gap alternative data fixes. A thin file isn't the same as a bad borrower; rejecting reliable payers just because they're new to credit is both unfair and bad business.

options.label: Quietly pull their health records and social posts too — more data is always better.
options.feedback: More data is not always better. Using sensitive data without consent or a lawful basis breaches privacy law (DPDP, GDPR, the Privacy Acts) and erodes trust. 'Can' is not 'should'.

interactions.title: Watch a clue change the verdict
interactions.prompt: Same Approval Dial — now focus on the bottom slider, 'Rent paid on time'. Hold the other sliders steady and drag just this alternative-data signal up and down. Watch a single new clue nudge the risk score and verdict. That's alternative data earning its keep.

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Lending runs on clues — old ones like your credit score, new ones like rent and behaviour — and the skill is gathering more clues, faster, without trampling people's privacy.”

checkpoint.q: Rent and utility payments used to assess a thin-file borrower are an example of…
checkpoint.explain: Alternative data = useful signals the old credit-score system ignored, like on-time rent and phone bills.

checkpoint.q: Customer-service call transcripts and social-media posts are best described as…
options: The same as a credit score
checkpoint.explain: They don't fit neat rows and columns — that's unstructured data, mined for early behavioural signals.

checkpoint.q: The most important guardrail when using alternative and unstructured data is…
options: Speed — gather it as fast as possible
Consent, fairness, and a lawful basis (privacy law)
Using as much data as technically possible
Keeping it secret from the borrower
checkpoint.explain: Privacy regimes (DPDP, GDPR, the Australian & NZ Privacy Acts, CDR) require consent and fair use. 'Can' isn't 'should'.
